question: Distinguish precisely among Starlink Subscriber, Service Line, and supported accounts. Which is most analogous to a revenue-generating customer, which has the highest count, and why?
facts: A Service Line is an individual instance of Starlink broadband internet service provisioned under a subscription plan, generally associated with a specific Starlink User Terminal (or group of terminals) and billed according to Starlink's service plans and terms of service.

Starlink Subscriber excludes managed enterprise and government customers with contracts in domains such as aviation, maritime, land mobility, fixed sites, and government entities.

Supported accounts refers to a human, bot, or similar account that logged into the X platform or Grok.
The total number of supported accounts may include fake, spam, or bot accounts if they are active.
The count of supported accounts was over 1.3 billion active in the twelve months ended March 31, 2026.
Starlink Subscriber is most analogous to a revenue-generating customer.
Each Starlink Subscriber represents a unique, billed Service Line — the atomic unit of subscription revenue generation.

For the recurring month-to-month contracts that characterize most broadband subscriptions, revenue recognized equals the amount billed in that month.
Supported accounts are not analogous to revenue-generating customers.
Supported accounts include bots and spam accounts.

The vast majority of supported accounts generate no direct revenue.
Supported accounts has by far the highest count at over 1.3 billion, compared to approximately 10.3 million Starlink Subscribers.
Supported accounts include any login — human, bot, or spam — requiring only a single login event in the trailing twelve months.
Supported accounts measures the entire X/Grok social media and AI platform user base, which inherently dwarfs the subscriber base of a paid satellite internet service.

Starlink Subscriber is the revenue-generating customer metric (billed consumer/small-business lines).

question: When evaluating future orbital AI compute satellites, which disclosed accounting policies or asset categories provide the closest precedent for treatment? Does the filing explicitly resolve whether such assets would be capitalized or expensed?
facts: The Satellites line item within Property, Plant, and Equipment is the most directly applicable accounting category for orbital AI compute satellites.

The disclosed Satellites policy states that satellites include costs to build the satellites (parts, labor, and allocated overhead) as well as capitalized launch costs incurred by the Space segment to launch the satellites to orbit (which includes an allocation of the flight vehicle).
Under the Satellites policy, build costs and launch costs are capitalized, not expensed.
Satellites have a useful life of 3–5 years.
Broadband satellites have a useful life of 5 years.
1st-generation mobile satellites have a useful life of 3 years.
Satellites are depreciated using the straight-line method.
As of March 31, 2026, the Satellites category carried $12,893 million gross.
As of March 31, 2026, Servers and networking equipment carried $23,850 million gross.
Servers and networking equipment have a useful life of 5–6 years.
The filing treats Satellites and Servers and networking equipment as separate, distinct PP&E line items.
The Starship program provides the closest analogy for how a development-stage space asset transitions from expensing to capitalization.
The Starship policy states that because it is in the development stage, a majority of Starship costs are expensed to Research and Development as incurred.
The Starship policy states that once Starship is commercialized by delivering payload to orbit, costs will generally be capitalized and then depreciated.
The general PP&E policy states that capitalization ceases when assets are substantially completed and ready for their intended use.
The filing does not explicitly resolve the accounting treatment for orbital AI compute satellites.

The filing defines AI compute satellite as a satellite equipped with onboard artificial intelligence processing capabilities designed to perform data analysis, inference, or other machine learning, automated decision-making and artificial intelligence algorithms, models and technologies workloads in orbit.
The filing targets deployment of orbital AI compute satellites as early as 2028.
The filing does not provide a specific accounting policy, classification, or capitalization/expensing determination for orbital AI compute satellites.
The filing does not address whether the AI compute function would cause these assets to be treated differently from traditional Starlink satellites.
The filing does not specify whether current R&D spending related to orbital AI compute satellite design is being tracked or expensed.
The filing notes no specific R&D costs for orbital AI compute satellites as of the filing date.
The filing supports the inference that pre-deployment costs would likely be expensed as R&D, following the Starship precedent.
The filing supports the inference that post-deployment costs would likely be capitalized as PP&E under the Satellites category, following the existing satellite capitalization policy.
The filing states the company expect[s] to allocate a significant amount of launch capacity to our AI segment in the future.
The filing does not address the tension between the 3–5 year satellite useful life range and compute hardware obsolescence.
The filing is silent on whether a new sub-category or line item might be warranted for the hybrid satellite-compute nature of the assets.

question: Orbital AI compute satellites would require inputs from multiple segments. What accounting-policy question would arise once manufacturing begins, and which disclosed policies appear most relevant?
facts: The primary accounting-policy question arising once Orbital AI compute satellite manufacturing begins is how inputs contributed by all three reportable segments should be measured, allocated, and eliminated when a single product draws on the Space, Connectivity, and AI segments simultaneously.
The existing intersegment policy addresses only a two-segment relationship (Space → Connectivity for Starlink launches).
Orbital AI compute satellites would involve all three segments contributing distinct, material inputs to a single asset.
The Space segment contributes Starship launch services, satellite platform manufacturing (V3 bus), and space-grade engineering and production capabilities to Orbital AI satellites.

The Connectivity segment contributes inter-satellite lasers and mesh networking technology, fleet management software, global downlink infrastructure, and in-orbit operational expertise to Orbital AI satellites.
The AI segment contributes AI accelerators and compute hardware, AI software/workloads (Grok), and terrestrial data center design expertise for modular satellite packaging to Orbital AI satellites.

The filing states the company "expect[s] to allocate a significant amount [of launch capacity] to our AI segment in the future" without specifying whether the same no-revenue, cost-capitalization approach will apply or whether a different transfer-pricing method will be used.
The filing contains no disclosed policy for how Connectivity-segment hardware or technology transferred to an AI-segment orbital asset would be priced or accounted for.
Currently, AI-segment servers and networking equipment are classified as PP&E within the AI segment and depreciated over 5–6 years.

The filing does not address whether AI compute hardware embedded in a satellite would remain an AI-segment asset, be reclassified as a Connectivity-segment satellite asset, or be treated as a shared asset requiring allocation.
The filing classifies Starlink satellites as Connectivity-segment PP&E ("Satellites" line item, $12.89 billion as of March 31, 2026).

The filing does not specify whether a hybrid asset like an Orbital AI compute satellite would be classified under "Satellites" (Connectivity), "Servers and networking equipment" (AI), or a new category.
The filing assigns different useful lives to different asset classes: satellites are depreciated over 3–5 years; servers and networking equipment over 5–6 years.

The filing does not specify which useful life would apply to an Orbital AI compute satellite, whether components would be depreciated separately, or whether a new useful-life estimate would be required.
The filing discloses that "shared cost allocation" for reusable launch vehicles and satellite production involves "significant estimates" and "significant management judgment."
For Starshield, the cost-to-cost input method requires estimation of "allocation of shared costs used in the production of satellites."
The filing does not address revenue attribution for a product that spans segments.
The company does not recognize intersegment revenue for internal Starlink launches; instead, launch costs are capitalized into PP&E.
Manufacturing costs (parts, labor, allocated overhead) and launch costs are capitalized into the "Satellites" PP&E line item.
The filing explicitly identifies the allocation of shared costs for reusable launch vehicles and satellite production as a critical estimate requiring significant management judgment.
Starshield contracts require estimation of "allocation of shared costs used in the production of satellites," including labor hours, material costs, and expected technological changes.
The filing notes that flight vehicles are depreciated based on expected flights rather than time.
The filing notes that "a majority of Starship costs are expensed to Research and Development as incurred, rather than capitalized as inventory," because Starship is still in development.
The S-1 filing does not provide sufficient information to resolve the intersegment transfer-pricing and cost-allocation question for Orbital AI compute satellites.
The existing intersegment policy uses a cost-pass-through approach without addressing arm's-length pricing, profit margins, or multi-segment contributions.

Once manufacturing begins, the company will need to establish and disclose policies for: (i) measuring and eliminating intersegment transfers across all three segments, (ii) determining which segment owns the resulting asset, (iii) selecting appropriate depreciation lives for the hybrid satellite-compute asset, and (iv) attributing revenue from orbital AI services to the correct segment(s).

question: What specific language does the filing use regarding timing of Starship commercial payload delivery, V3 satellite deployment, and V2 Mobile deployment? Compare the wording and assess what it suggests about execution and timing risk.
facts: The filing states SpaceX expects Starship to commence payload delivery to orbit in the second half of 2026 following additional flight tests.
The filing states SpaceX expects to commence deploying next-generation V3 satellites using Starship in the second half of 2026.
The filing states SpaceX plans to expand its mobile constellation by deploying next-generation V2 Mobile satellites in 2027.
Starship payload delivery uses the qualifier "expect".
V3 satellite deployment uses the qualifier "expect".
V2 Mobile deployment uses the qualifier "plan".
The qualifier "plan" denotes intention rather than expectation of outcome, making it a materially weaker qualifier than "expect".
The timeframe for Starship payload delivery is specified as "second half of 2026".
The timeframe for V3 satellite deployment is specified as "second half of 2026".
The timeframe for V2 Mobile deployment is specified only as "2027" with no half-year specification.
The language moves from "expect" with a specific half-year timeframe to "plan" with only a calendar-year timeframe, reflecting increasing uncertainty as the dependency chain lengthens.
Falcon 9 and Falcon Heavy are not capable of deploying V3 satellites and V2 Mobile satellites.
V3 and V2 Mobile deployment are entirely contingent on Starship success with no fallback launch vehicle.
Any Starship delay cascades directly into V3 and V2 Mobile timelines.
The "expect" language for Starship and V3 is explicitly conditioned on "additional flight tests" and "continued successful iteration".
Full reusability of Starship's upper stage is not required to deploy V3 satellites.
Full reusability is not required for V2 Mobile deployment.
The explicit statement that full upper-stage reusability is not required for V3 or V2 Mobile deployment is a meaningful risk mitigation.
The risk factors state there can be no assurance that SpaceX will be able to achieve its objectives with respect to Starship within the expected timeframes, if at all.
The risk factors state SpaceX's ability to execute its growth strategy is highly dependent on Starship.
The filing states SpaceX cannot predict when or whether international regulatory approvals will be granted for V2 Mobile in any given market.

V2 Mobile carries the widest cone of uncertainty due to the weaker "plan" qualifier, the vaguer timeframe, the multi-layered dependency chain, and the explicit admission of inability to predict international approval timelines.
The gap between aspirational timelines and candid risk disclosure is indicative of significant execution and timing risk across all three programs.
